title: 2026 03 04 ISC
description: Wednesday 4 March 2026 | Intelligence and Security Committee 2024/25 Annual reviews of the New Zealand Security Intelligence Service and the Government Communications Security Bureau Government Communications Security Bureau Andrew Clark, Director-General New Zealand Security Intelligence Service Andrew Hampton, Director-General

chunks.text: Gentlemen for being here with us today, and I open it up to you to invite, uh, to make some opening interrupting remarks. Uh, thank you Prime Minister, uh, afternoon committee members. Uh, thanks for the opportunity to provide an update on the work of the GCSB. I have a few opening comments to offer, and then I'll hand to, uh, Andrew for some New Zealand SIS comments, and then we're happy to take any, any questions.

Great, thank you guys. Uh, starting globally. Over the past year, the volatile Geostrategic environment and the rapid pace of disruptive technologies have been key factors shaping our core business, which is delivering signals intelligence and building cyber resilience. For New Zealand, that unpredictability is, it's effectively moving the goalposts of what resilience looks like, and it requires a more proactive approach to detecting and disrupting threats and close cooperation with others.

In this changing environment, we've, uh, provided intelligence relating to terrorist activity and to foreign state activity where that could threaten the safety of New Zealanders and international partners. Conflict intentions have sometimes arisen with little notice, and this week's major conflict in the Middle East is no exception.

And our team has been providing round the clock threat intelligence updates to our customers, especially to the NZDF and closer to home. We provided intelligence about geostrategic and military activity that affects the security of our region and New Zealand more directly.

We also boosted our impact on transnational organized crime in the Pacific through a dedicated mission under Operation Kiwi with customs and the NZDF and in close cooperation with regional partners. And just this year, it's already led into the seizure of several tons of narcotics at sea.

Domestically, we supported agencies including the New Zealand SIS and their efforts to counter terrorists activity and foreign interference. Turning to cybersecurity, the geostrategic environment and the pace of technology are again, key factors shaping our cyber threat scape. The National Cybersecurity Center recently highlighted five key themes of New Zealand's current environment.

First, that foreign state sponsored actors continue to be active and targeting our country's networks. Second, that cyber criminals are accessing technologies that allow them to essentially commercialize their activities at a greater scale. Third, that issues motivated hacktivists are targeting New Zealand organizations, including a byproduct of global conflicts.

Fourth, that cyber threat actors are increasingly targeting organizations through their supply chains. And finally, but importantly, it is known weaknesses and unpatched vulnerabilities in organizations, networks that continue to provide threat actors with easy access. In many cases in recent New Zealand cyber incidents, including the attacks on manage my health and medimap, uh, fit within those themes.

While the NCSC uses several tools to detect and defeat threats, including cortex and also, uh, malware free networks, which recently clicked over 1 billion disruptions of threats to the country's networks, these tools are having to adapt as technologies and threats change. Of course, we have never been a national firewall, and so everyone in the national cybersecurity ecosystem has a part to play.

Over the past year, we've strengthened our engagement with critical infrastructure providers who are becoming higher profile targets for malicious actors. Consultation with these key organizations is now underway about potential cybersecurity regulation. We're also increasing our outreach to South Pacific partners to help lift cyber resilience across the region.

In terms of our own resilience, I'm pleased to advise the committee that in June, the new, uh, all of government data center named MATA was opened at NUI in Auckland on the financial front. GCSB has been managing sustainably within its budget, largely absorbing cost pressures to date.

Although I do note the competition for talent is getting stronger. Looking ahead, the rapid pace of technology development is going to present both security challenges and opportunities. We're already seeing that with ai, but I expect it to happen also in the areas of quantum computing and cryptography.

Some shifts may be hard to predict and could occur at short notice, meaning that, uh, we may have to adapt quickly. So being both proactive and agile is going to be important in that environment. Collaborative problem solving with our close international partners will make all the difference, uh, as of course will talented people at GCSB that I'm very fortunate to lead.

I'll now hand over to Andrew. Um, thank you, Andrew. I thank you for the opportunity to talk with you today. Almost three months on from the horrific terrorist attack on Bondi Beach on 14 December. Our thoughts are very much with the Jewish communities on both sides of the Tasman.

At the N-Z-S-I-S, we immediately stood up an operation to understand how the Bondi attack might impact the New Zealand threat environment. We also provided support as required to our Australian and to our New Zealand police colleagues. The operation ran right through the summer break, and we continue to receive relevant intelligence.

The N-Z-S-I-S is also monitoring the unfolding situation in Iran and the potential impact on our local threat environment. We do not see any change to the current threat profile, although it is still early days. The Bondi attack has unfortunately resonated with violent extremists from across the ideological spectrum.

Faith-based extremist organizations like ISIS and Al-Qaeda paid close attention, so too did identity based violent extremists who freely promote vile, antisemitic, and Islamic phobic narratives. The Bondi attack also helped to feed and motivate anti-immigration narratives that are used by some politically motivated violent extremists.

Another concerning dimension was the use of AI to falsify and, um, spread online content about the attack. We were all shocked and appalled by the senseless attack at Bondi, but unfortunately, I was not completely surprised. This is the type of low capability without warning attack.

The N-Z-S-A-S frequently warns could happen here that could come from violent extremists from across the ideological spectrum. The Combined Threat Assessment Group inside N-Z-S-I-S has recently updated the descriptors used for the national terrorism threat level to better reflect the nature of the threat environment.

A terrorist attack remains a realistic possibility in New Zealand that is now reflected on the scale as possible rather than low as the level was previously described. Using the term possible is intended to focus minds on the need to be prepared. Both anti sem, both anti-Semitism and Islamophobia are diseases we see running through the violent extremist environment in New Zealand.

Often alongside other dark motivations, new Zealanders should be alert that it is possible someone with extreme antisemitic Islamic phobic views may develop the intent and capability to conduct an attack. Faith-based and ethnic communities feel this threat deeply and are increasingly concerned with the level of hate they face.

I heard these concerns firsthand in my engagement with the Jewish and Muslim communities in just the month before the Bondi attack. While my agency and police are very focused on detecting violent extremists, we are not all seen and all known, and nor should we be in a democracy like ours.

New Zealanders are just as likely to see concerning behavior as we are. The right thing to do is to report that behavior to us or to the police. The broader domestic threat environment also presents a range of challenges including foreign interference, espionage, and insiders within organizations who wittingly or unwittingly can harm our national security.

The N-Z-S-I-S is intensifying efforts to promote a stronger security mindset across a board range of sectors in our society, including through our annual security threat environment report, new Zealand's relationships, our innovation, our location in the South Pacific and our close proximity to Antarctica, all make New Zealand geo strategically significant.

This has been positive for New Zealand's international reputation and our economic prosperity, but that geostrategic significance also brings with it some downstream threats to our national security. There continues to be a number of states that conduct foreign interference in espionage in New Zealand.

This is not benign activity. Such actors have a voracious appetite for covert influence and non-public information that they can leverage to gain an advantage over us and our partners. For example, increasingly we see influential New Zealanders or people with access to privileged information being targeted by foreign intelligence services through deceptive online recruitment.

Certain states also want us to line up with their talking points and ensure our communities and civil society don't speak out of turn. Deception, coercion and corruption are frequently used to further their goals in ways that are contrary to our national interest. The fact that we now have criminal offenses for such activities reinforces that foreign interference and espionage is unacceptable In New Zealand.

The N-Z-S-I-S continues to have success disrupting foreign interference and espionage activity, but what we see may just be the tip of the iceberg. As always, prevention will be the best defense, and there is much that organizations and communities can do to manage the risk. We have some excellent advice and guidance, but leaders of organizations, businesses and communities also need to take steps to protect their people, their critical information and their assets.

Um, finally, I'd like to acknowledge, um, the extraordinary professionalism and dedication shown by the people of, uh, N-Z-S-I-S who I'm proud to lead every day. Um, prime Minister, minister Collins, um, you both had the opportunity over the past year to see up close impressive work they do.

And Minister Collins, on behalf of both agencies, we'd like to thank you for the leadership and support you have provided to our two agencies as our minister over the last, um, two and a bit views. Um, thank you. We're now happy to take questions. Well, thank you gentlemen, and thank you. Um, uh, again, we joined all of us here and thank the people that are working in your Organizations. I appreciate those opening remarks.

Um, we're gonna open it up to the floor. We need to close by 4 55. Can I just lead off and then I'll pass to Chris and then to Priyanka. Um, what, what is the level of maturity in GCSB question? So what is the level of maturity that you're observing around cybersecurity across New Zealand, and how do you think we are placed with respect to critical infrastructure?

And even maybe a quick comment on public service. Yeah. Uh, thanks, prime Minister. Um, the, I would describe across New Zealand as as highly variable. Um, uh, we have some, uh, pockets of excellent cybersecurity, um, and including in some of, um, our most critical critical infrastructure in the areas that we would, uh, we would hope to have it.

Yeah. Uh, unfortunately there are, um, there are also pockets, including in our critical infrastructure, uh, where that cybersecurity is barely meeting a, a foundational, uh, level that, that we would expect in the public sector. Uh, we've seen the level of cybersecurity maturity continue to, to lift, um, but the bad guys always try and find the weakest point to attack next.

And currently what we're seeing is that that is through supply chains, including digital supply chains. So what we're now having to pay more attention to is some of those, uh, contracted suppliers, for example, to the public sector, and make sure that the level of cybersecurity maturity that they're providing is what we would expect for the public sector itself.

Yes. Uh, just in terms of, uh, critical infrastructure providers, I'll just note that the consultation with critical infrastructure organizations started, uh, just last week, uh, in terms of what more formal expectations might look like around cybersecurity standards setting. Yeah. Excellent, Chris. Mm-hmm. Thank you.

Um, Andrew Hampton, I, I guess, uh, any members of the public watching this on the live stream, hearing your comments around the sort of direct level to New Zealand would probably be quite alarmed and maybe scared by some of the comments that, that you have made. And I think it's probably speaks to a wider anxiety about the state of the world that many people will be feeling at the moment.

My question to you would be, what are you asking New Zealanders to do with that information? Yeah, because, uh, if you are listening in and, and this is the first thing you are hearing, this, you will be scared by what you've just said. Yeah. So what are you asking people to do do with that information? Um, thank you, Mr. Hopkins. Firstly, um, our intention is never to alarm people.

It's to alert them to the risk, but also to help point them in the direction of what steps they, they can take. Um, as a result of a range of factors, the global environment increased polarization online, um, more young people engaging with violent content online means that the environment that we are facing certainly has more complexity than we've had before.

You know, there's a range of different motivations. There's people shopping between, between different motivations, and there's also the, um, ability for people to go quite a long way down that radicalization path by just engaging on a computer. So that is concerning. Um, also concerning, obviously New Zealand's had two recent terrorist attacks.

We've had their attack and Bondi. So these, these things do, do happen. We're not immune to it. So what do we want communities to do, or all, all citizens to do? Look, the first thing is to, um, just be aware of the signs that someone may be vulnerable or on a pathway to, to radicalization.

What we've found through looking at cases is as often their signs are, they're quite, quite early, um, particularly with people's online behavior, but also engaging in those types of narratives. And the earlier organizations like us in place and the wider social sector become aware of them, the more options there are to, to do something about it.

So we produced a booklet a few years ago, as you know, called Know the Signs. It's available online. It talks about what some of those signs are. We're doing a lot of work in the education sector now around sharing that. So the first thing is public just to be aware of, you know, what those concerning behaviors are. And then they need to have the trust and confidence, and that's a big part of what we do.

When they see stuff to raise it with us or the the police, we may be actually able to assess that there isn't, isn't a risk of that person while, uh, uh, not a high risk of that person mobilizing to violence at the same time, it could be the missing part of a part of a puzzle. So that's one of the things that I'm keen to do at a very pragmatic level.

I'm keen for parents to take a stronger interest in what their kids are looking at online. I, I've said this before, but if you, if you look at our current active investigations, and I've shared with that with some committee members, it looks like a year 13 boys school and the diversity that you would see there, there's not a single ideology, there's not a single ethnicity.

But what's common is these are largely people who are spending a lot of time online engaging with quite violent, violent content. Um, unfortunately, they are also often people who have other vulnerabilities around, um, um, drug and alcohol and things like that, which reinforces the need for it to be a all of society approach.

So a long answer there, but I think the key things be aware it could be happen here. Be aware of the signs of someone who's could be mobilizing, tell us about it, but also keep an eye on what your kids are doing. I mean, I dunno whether this is a question which, which Andrew, this is a question four, but in terms of that online risk for parents, I think that is something parents are highly attuned to at the moment.

What steps are you taking to alert parents to the fact that that risk might not be in places where they think it is? Yeah, so social media, I think everyone's very attuned to, but there are video games that kids are playing Yeah. That are connected, that, that can be used, uh, by those with a malicious intent. Yeah. And our parents are aware enough of those sorts of risks. Look, Um, we, we can't be be everywhere.

So opportunities like this are valuable to, to raise it. There is material on our, our website around what those signs are. But what we are also doing is partnering with others, partnering with people in the, um, education sector. Had an excellent meeting with the senior team at education recently, and they point us in the direction of, um, who are the, um, people who can get those messages out to teachers.

We went with principals. So I think the education sector is part of it. Others we've partnered with though are the disability sector, because sometimes, um, individuals who for whatever reason are, are isolated, spend more time online, may be more vulnerable, um, are from that sector.

So they have actually taken some of the material and developed a training program for people in that sector. So a big part of it's doing that. The last thing I would say, which we are very pleased about, because we didn't actually initiate it happens, but if you buy a, an Xbox in New Zealand, now the appearance guide includes a link to link to, um, our guidance guys.

Um, can we go to Priyanka and then Tiana Or do you wanna go the other way? Uh, well, I just had a quick set. Yeah. Um, so I was just sort of interested in your comments and I wonder if you had one around misinformation and disinformation. Um, particularly when you're looking at social media. Uh, social media platforms as well. I mean, parents and teachers have gotta sleep, but algorithms, dope.

Um, so got a look. Getting work here should be focusing, You know, our agencies aren't and shouldn't be the internet place. You know, we have, we have broad, broad, broad powers to undertake investigations into people who could be national security threats.

I think it would be wrong for us to have some broader role in placing content on the internet. We can raise awareness of it. Um, but there, there are a couple of things I would, I would say there is, yeah, with without a doubt, um, the polarizing and grievance based rhetoric that we see online.

Much of it generated from overseas as driving people towards fine extremist narratives. You know, that, that's proven now. And I think it's bigger than our country. It's a, it's a global issue. I think the second thing though, and you touched on algorithms, they are a factor. But what we are finding with these young people, they're actually going looking for this material.

It's not just being pushed at them. Sometimes they're going looking for it. And I, I don't, I don't want to lump it all on parents 'cause I know how hard it can be. But, um, there's been examples where, you know, children we've, or young people we've become aware of or police become aware of, we go and engage with the parents or the police.

Do we tend not to, the parents had no idea what their, their kids are up to. Bianca, Thank you. Um, my questions are also sort of, um, against the backdrop of the comments that you made around the terrorism level, moving from low to possible. Yeah. You've talked about the need to be prepared.

Um, you've both also mentioned that you maximize your impact to keep New Zealand safe through different relationships and networks that you are part of. Um, I want to ask you about your involvement in a particular decision. You'll be aware that at the 21 may meeting of the National Security Board, um, that the board, including representation from both your agencies Yeah.

Unanimously recommended that the terrorism designation of the American Proud Boys be renewed. Um, you will be aware that there was further advice given, um, uh, by the CE of DPMC that it not be renewed, and that was subsequently allowed, um, to, to lapse. Now, is that change in advice, something that was endorsed by the National Security Board, including yourselves?

Um, if I, if I go back to the original decision to designate the Proud Boys in 2022, um, CT a, the, um, combined Threat Assessment Group, which is hosted by, um, N-Z-S-A-S, we didn't strongly support it at that point. We didn't think they actually met the threshold, but at that point, the view generally was they should be recognized when it came around.

This time, we didn't have a strong view either. We had questions about whether they actually met the, met the threshold initially when it went up, but yep, I was part of that decision to endorse it. The fact is, for me, the proud boys are not a priority for us in terms of our, our investigations. So whilst there was that, I was part of that decision that that went up, um, Ben reviewed it, he raised some points.

I was comfortable with the points that he raised because in some ways they were in line with the advice that CAG had actually given back in 20 20 22. So as far as you're aware, that did go back to the National Security Board for endorsement, that changing? No, no. It was, Ben is, well, Ben can speak for himself, but Ben is chair of the National Security Board.

He made the decision, but he advised us of his reasons. And as I said, I, I know I'm sounding a little bit ambivalent here, but we didn't necessarily think it was a, a strongly supported decision. First time I went around with the second time, the reality is it's not making much difference to the New Zealand threat environment because they aren't, um, subjects for our investigation.

Okay. Uh, the reason I ask that is because Mr. King did say to a parliamentary select committee that the board has endorsed that changed he position. So you would've endorsed that despite police saying that they are a crypto fascist group with participation in New Zealand. Yeah. Look, um, we are now out of the classified, um, environment where I could be, this is all in the public domain I know, but I could talk a bit more around, there was a lot of debate that my predecessor was in in 2022 about whether they should be recognized.

And she was not strongly supportive of it. The CT a's advice was not a whole lot had, had, had changed. So I guess what I'm saying is, yep, I, I was part of that initial decision, but I was also comfortable with where it ended up because we, it ended up, it was probably close to our original advice. Either way, it doesn't make much difference to my work in terms of our investigations 'cause they're, they're not a, a priority focus for investigations here.

Okay. Brooke? Uh, yes. Look, I'm quite concerned, I think both as a government minister and as an indi individual Yeah. About, um, cybersecurity threats and, uh, the, I guess the, the relaxed nature in which some companies are holding people's data and not having very secure platforms for that information being stored.

And I wonder, you know, over where we are, um, and the year ahead where you would hope to see some of those New Zealand based companies actually end up, and do you actually have a plan or engagements with companies and businesses to help, you know, increase their, their privacy and security systems?

Uh, yes. Thank you Minister. The, uh, so yes, and, and yes. Um, I think we've already covered off the, the critical infrastructure question. So there's a already that, uh, more formal process to look at how we, uh, set and enforce cybersecurity standards across, um, critical infrastructure.

Uh, but of course, many, many organizations are not, uh, critical infrastructure and many organizations, um, some of 'em quite small hold, uh, really sensitive personal information belonging to, uh, to New Zealanders. Uh, so as part of the, uh, action plan for the, the latest national cybersecurity strategy, it was, uh, recently released.

There's a couple of lines in there that talk about, uh, the question of, uh, essentially privacy. Um, because, uh, because what, in my view, what, uh, is is a, a missing piece at the moment, is the right incentives in place, uh, for organizations that hold highly sensitive data to, uh, to have what we would expect would be the right levels of cybersecurity in place to be able to hold that really sensitive, uh, information.

As we look across some of our like-minded, uh, countries, uh, nearby in, in Europe and, and others, uh, we can see that as the, the goalposts have shifted in cybersecurity expectations, um, that they have updated some of their settings and some of those incentives have been put in place.

So under this element of the cybersecurity strategy now, uh, we will work with other agencies to put up advice on, uh, what, what we think needs to change in that area. Mm-hmm. Good. Um, any other questions? Yeah, Sorry. Yeah. Um, um, so yeah, I've been thinking about, uh, you know, the US Israel struts and Iran, and, you know, this is a terrifying moment in history for particularly people in the Middle East, but also their families here, um, as well.

And, and I wonder if you could provide us some clarity and, and for people, um, uh, watching us around the, the, the, the nature of the relationship with Five Ice Partners, uh, the parameters that you have to operate in, um, uh, I think that would give more, um, certainty or clarity for people in terms of the role that you play, uh, in terms of that, uh, certainly for some of us on outside of the house, the relationship, uh, the close, the potential closeness with the Israel in particular is, is problematic.

And so maybe you could explain using that moment as a, as an example of, of your role. Um, in this, Maybe I'll just talk very generally about the intelligence sharing relationship we have within the, and then I'll hand over to Andrew to talk specifically, um, about current events. Look, um, New Zealand gets significant benefit from a range of intelligence relationships.

Five eyes in particular. We receive indispensable intelligence and technical advice that we just couldn't get, um, from anyone else. The key point we want to emphasize though, is the engagement that New Zealand has with our international partners is on our own terms. We, um, cannot ask them to do anything we couldn't legally do ourselves and vice versa.

There are clear policies and processes in place to ensure that everything all sharing or cooperation is done in accordance with New Zealand law, including human rights, um, requirements. We need to, um, do regular human rights assessments of all parties who we cooperate with, including our Five Eyes partners in order to to, to work with them.

So yes, we have, um, a need to be vigilant to get the benefit of that intelligence sharing relationship, but in a way that does not compromise New Zealand's law or law or values. And I'll hand to Andrew, who it's a very real thing for him as a second agency.

Uh, yes. Uh, thanks Mr. Tian. Ano, um, as a second sharing agency, we are, we are sharing, uh, intelligence every day of every week with our, with our Five Eyes Partners. Um, uh, the intelligence value for New Zealand is, is, uh, really significant, um, from, um, intelligence about threats to cyber threats to New Zealand's, uh, networks, uh, to terrorists threats, um, uh, to organize organized crime.

Uh, we get a a lot out of that. Um, but there are important, uh, provisions built into the machinery and around five eyes, uh, shearing. Uh, and that is that, um, uh, we have good control around, uh, whether intelligence, uh, uh, to withhold intelligence, um, when it, uh, might compromise New Zealand's own legal, uh, settings, our own, uh, human rights settings, uh, our own policy settings.

And so not everything is shared, uh, all the time, and that is part of the machinery of, of Five Eyes. And I think that's one of the why, one of the reasons that actually it can continues to, to endure, um, because it has those safeguards, uh, built into it. Um, and that includes even, uh, where one of the partners might want to share some of our intelligence with another partner outside of the, the Five Eyes.

Those same, uh, controls and settings and provisions apply. Uh, and we will, we will do the consideration, uh, of whether those requests, um, meet New Zealand's own, own settings or not. And of course, we have an independent inspector general who gets to see everything that we do. He has taken a, a strong interest in our cooperation with, um, international partners.

He, he has it under, under constant, um, constant review. Um, and so that's an extra check for, um, for New Zealanders as well. And, and just a quick follow up and, and will that follow through with the data center, the ma data center as well? Uh, and I, I mean, I sort of get the ease with interoperability, but then it can come with some of these, um, challenges that you've described.

Yes. You know, that is a sovereign, uh, capability, uh, for us and also comes within the purview of the Inspector General. Okay. Well, uh, gentlemen, can I just say thank you very much for, um, uh, engaging with us today. Really appreciate it. Uh, we're gonna conclude the questioning now. Um, the public, the recording will, uh, conclude as well. Um, the session will return to private, and if I can ask everybody to leave the room, that would be great.
